# Research: `badlogic/pibot` ("Pipi")

A study of Mario Zechner's (badlogic) personal voice robot, built on the **same
`pi` agent kernel orbit embeds** (`@earendil-works/pi-agent-core`). Because the
brain is identical, this is an unusually clean comparison: same agent loop,
different everything-else. The reason to read it is the real-time conversational
edges — STT gating, barge-in, vision — against orbit's own.

## Provenance

| | |
|---|---|
| **Subject** | `badlogic/pibot` — https://github.com/badlogic/pibot |
| **Subject commit** | `2ef6b692a3aeb0f4e6e9c0db74c83923cf3d5b7e` (2026-06-07) — "Update qwen3_tts_rs submodule" |
| **Orbit commit** | `60b2b4e4a2563ab0ee202999d274c850b48d7a28` on branch `last30days-research-tool` (2026-06-21) — "consensus capture for force_get_current" |
| **Researched** | 2026-06-22 |
| **Analyst** | Claude (Opus 4.8) + guru, interactive session |
| **Method** | Cloned pibot to a temp dir and **read the source** (server, client, native STT worker, build scripts). Read orbit's **real** perception/brain code + docs. **No code was run; no benchmarks were executed** on either side. See Method & honesty. |

## Method & honesty

Be clear about what backs the findings below, because a wrong provenance claim
poisons every takeaway.

- **pibot**: `git clone --depth 1` into `/tmp`, then read source. I read
  [`src/server/index.ts`](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/server/index.ts)
  (the voice-loop state machine), `harness.ts` (pi wiring), `src/client/barge-in.ts`
  (the echo canceller), `native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp` (the STT worker),
  `tts.ts`/`stt.ts`/`llama.ts` (the native-worker plumbing), the client tools, and
  the build scripts. **I did NOT run pibot, load its models, or benchmark STT/TTS/LLM.**
  Latency/quality numbers quoted from pibot are its own README/source claims, not
  measured by me.
- **orbit**: I read the **actual code** — most importantly
  [stt-watch.ts](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts)
  in full — plus [perception-pipeline.md](../perception-pipeline.md),
  [perception-to-brain.md](../perception-to-brain.md), and
  [server-brain-impl.md](../decision-traces/server-brain-impl.md). An earlier draft
  of this analysis wrongly claimed orbit was "app-side heavy"; reading the real code
  corrected that (orbit is server-heavy — see below). The corrected version is what's
  recorded here.
- **What's NOT validated**: no A/B, no latency measurement, no head-to-head STT
  accuracy test. Takeaways are **source-reading + architectural reasoning**, not
  empirical. Where a takeaway needs a measurement to act on, it says so.

## Things to note / takeaways

The spine of this entry. Each is a claim + confidence + verdict + cites, so you can
act without re-reading the supporting analysis. Verdict legend:
**steal** (worth adopting) · **skip** (don't) · **have** (orbit already does this) ·
**watch** (a question to keep in mind).

| # | Takeaway | Verdict | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Both projects make the **same topology bet** — thin client, server does the ML. "Orbit is app-heavy" was wrong. | have | high |
| 2 | pibot's barge-in is a **JS cross-correlation echo canceller**, NOT magic. Orbit's hardware-AEC path (`USAGE_VOICE_COMMUNICATION`) is strictly better. | skip | high |
| 3 | **Preroll-on-interrupt**: when barge-in fires, pibot ships the already-buffered mic audio so the first words of the interruption aren't lost. | watch | medium |
| 4 | pibot's STT reliability is **conservative gating around the recognizer**, not a better model. Orbit already does the same shape — and more. | have | high |
| 5 | **Energy pre-gate in front of the neural VAD** — pibot refuses to run Silero on near-silent frames. Moot for orbit (orbit's VAD *is* an RMS gate). | skip (moot) | high |
| 6 | Orbit's STT **confidence gating is more advanced** than pibot's (Whisper logprob/no_speech/compression, tiered good/shaky/garbage). pibot has none of this. | have | high |
| 7 | pibot's vision is naive (one on-demand photo → VLM). **Orbit is strictly ahead** (consensus capture, 512px lever, anti-anchor prompt, motion gating). | have | high |
| 8 | On-demand-only vision is a valid, **cheaper-to-make-reliable** posture vs orbit's continuous pipeline. Product trade-off, not a bug. | watch | medium |
| 9 | **Browser was the right thing to reject** for orbit: a tab can't do reliable always-on or hardware-AEC TTS; pibot sidesteps both by being a plugged-in foreground tab. | have | high |
| 10 | `createLocalModel` + `PI_PROVIDER`/`PI_MODEL` env swap lets the **same pi harness** target local llama.cpp OR cloud with one var — a clean offline-fallback path. | steal | medium |
| 11 | `beforeTool` **gates tool execution on TTS finishing** (say "let me look" → *then* the camera fires), and aborts the tool if a barge-in lands during the wait. | steal | medium |
| 12 | Empty-assistant-response **auto-continuation** prompt — a workaround for weak *local* models. A tell of what local LLMs cost in robustness; orbit (cloud) doesn't need it. | skip | high |
| 13 | **Parakeet STT** (TDT 0.6B, int8 ONNX, ~50× RT) is a real accuracy+speed upgrade over Whisper-small.en — BUT only 25 European languages (no Hindi/Indic). The bigger lever is his **interim-every-250ms streaming**, portable to any engine. | watch | high |
| 14 | **"Pocket TTS" (Qwen3-TTS)** runs locally on a *Mac*, NOT on the *phone*. Better voice + cloning. **AEC is NOT lost** (orbit already renders TTS PCM through WebRTC's ADM = the AEC reference). Real cost is **station GPU contention** with the continuous vision loop (serialized single Metal GPU) — not RAM, not AEC. Measure synth latency under live vision. | watch | high |

### The two that are actually actionable

- **#3 preroll-on-interrupt** ([barge-in.ts:71,90-98](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/client/barge-in.ts#L90)):
  Orbit's *STT* path already keeps a 200 ms onset preroll ([stt-watch.ts:200-212](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts#L200)),
  but that's a different thing — pibot's preroll is on the **barge-in/interrupt**
  path, preserving the leading syllables of an interruption ("stop— no, I meant…").
  **Action**: check whether orbit's barge-in / voice-interrupt path drops the first
  words of an interrupting utterance. If it does, a small mic ring on that path fixes
  it. Needs a real test to confirm it's a problem.
- **#11 speech-gated tools** ([index.ts:380-387](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/server/index.ts#L380), via pi's `on("tool_call")` hook):
  Clean ordering of speak-vs-act. Worth checking whether orbit's brain already
  orders the spoken acknowledgement before the tool side-effect; if not, this is a
  small, high-polish win.

Everything else is either **already done better in orbit** (#1, #4, #5, #6, #7, #9)
or a **deliberate non-goal** (#2, #12) or a **trade-off to keep in mind** (#8, #10).

## Supporting analysis

### Architecture: both are server-heavy (corrects the earlier mistake)

| | **pibot** | **orbit** |
|---|---|---|
| Client | Browser tab (is the face/mic/speaker/camera/motor driver) | Android app (capture + TTS + face UI) |
| LLM | **Local** llama.cpp (Gemma 4 26B-A4B MoE Q4, vision mmproj) | **Cloud** Gemini/OpenRouter via pi |
| STT | **Local** parakeet.cpp + Silero VAD (server child proc) | **Local** Whisper MLX sidecar (:8078) + own RMS-VAD |
| TTS | **Local** qwen3-tts (server) → browser plays streamed PCM | **Android** TextToSpeech on the phone |
| Vision | `take_photo` on demand → multimodal llama.cpp | **Continuous** Qwen2.5-VL sidecar (:8080) + face-api |
| Multi-device | No (one browser session per user) | Yes (docks registry, capability routing) |

Both push the heavy ML to a server and keep the client thin. The real divergences:
**client substrate** (browser vs Android), **LLM location** (local vs cloud), **TTS
location** (server-PCM vs on-device Android), and **vision posture** (on-demand vs
continuous). The "orbit is app-heavy" framing in the first draft was wrong — orbit
*deleted* on-phone STT and the on-phone agent in the server-brain cutover
([server-brain-impl.md](../decision-traces/server-brain-impl.md)); the phone now only
captures A/V (WebRTC Opus/VP8), plays Android TTS, and renders the face.

### Barge-in: a heuristic echo canceller, not magic (#2, #3)

[`src/client/barge-in.ts`](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/client/barge-in.ts)
runs a **software AEC by cross-correlation** because pibot *disables* the browser's
built-in echo cancellation. While the robot speaks it keeps a ring of the PCM it's
playing ([L41-53](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/client/barge-in.ts#L41)),
and per mic frame sweeps a **20–420 ms delay window**
([L114](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/client/barge-in.ts#L114)),
correlating mic against delayed playback to compute a **residual ratio** = fraction
of mic energy NOT explained by the playback
([L128-129](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/client/barge-in.ts#L128)).
Pure echo → residual ≈ 0 (ignore); real speech over the robot's voice → residual ≈ 1
(interrupt). Fires when `rms ≥ 0.018` AND `residual ≥ 0.62` for 5 consecutive frames.

Orbit gets the same effect for free in hardware: A1.2 routes TTS through
`USAGE_VOICE_COMMUNICATION` so the phone's **DSP echo canceller** sees mic+speaker
and cancels in silicon — lower latency, no CPU, no tuning constants. The orbit
echo-*gate* (drop mic while speaking) is even DEFAULT-OFF now precisely because the
hardware AEC makes it redundant and it would block voice barge-in
([stt-watch.ts:30-33,480-489](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts#L30)).
So: **don't port the echo math.** The one transferable idea is the *preroll on
interrupt* (#3) and the "skip AEC analysis when the robot is silent" gate
([barge-in.ts:65](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/client/barge-in.ts#L65)).

### STT: orbit already does the gating shape, and more (#4, #5, #6)

pibot's STT worker [`native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp`](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp)
gets its reliability from **conservative gates around the recognizer**, not the model:
1. **Energy pre-gate before the neural VAD** ([main.cpp:190](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp#L190)):
   won't run Silero on frames below `energy_gate=0.002`.
2. **Preroll prepend** ([main.cpp:192-198](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp#L192)):
   1800 ms ring prepended at speech onset so the first word survives.
3. **Min-utterance drop** ([main.cpp:221-222](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp#L221)):
   sub-450 ms blips dropped pre-recognition with a `speech_drop` event.
4. **VAD reset per utterance** ([main.cpp:233](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/native/parakeet-cpp-stt/src/main.cpp#L233)).

Cross-checked against the **actual** orbit
[stt-watch.ts](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts):
- **Preroll prepend — orbit ALREADY HAS IT** ([L200-212](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts#L200)):
  `PREROLL_MS=200`, prepended at onset (`this.#utter = [...this.#preroll, frame]`).
  (pibot's 1800 ms is far larger, but pibot's is also its whole speech-detection
  preroll, not just an onset guard — different role.)
- **Min-utterance drop — HAS IT** (`MIN_UTTERANCE_MS=180`, [L219](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts#L219)).
- **Energy pre-gate (#5) — MOOT**: orbit doesn't use a neural VAD; its VAD *is* an
  RMS threshold (`SILENCE_RMS=0.02`, [L40,L193](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts#L40)).
  pibot's pre-gate exists to avoid *running Silero* on silence — orbit has no Silero
  to protect, so there's nothing to port.
- **Confidence gating (#6) — orbit is well AHEAD**: it reads Whisper's OWN
  `avg_logprob` / `no_speech_prob` / `compression_ratio`
  ([L257-347](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts#L257)),
  tiers transcripts `good`/`shaky`/`garbage` aligned to Whisper's documented silence
  rule (logprob AND no_speech together), detects repetition-loop hallucinations, and
  keeps a phrase backstop ("Thank you"/"you"/beep artifacts) so the dock never replies
  to its own silence-hallucinations. pibot's worker has **none** of this — it emits raw
  parakeet text. **orbit's STT reliability work is materially more sophisticated than
  pibot's.** If orbit STT still feels unreliable, the lever is upstream of pibot's
  ideas (model choice, far-field audio, sidecar quality), not these gates.

### Vision: orbit is strictly ahead (#7, #8)

pibot vision = one `take_photo` JPEG → the multimodal llama.cpp model, on demand. No
consensus, no multi-frame, no anti-hallucination prompt, no resolution tuning, no
camera-motion gating. Orbit has all of those (512px-is-the-lever, 3-frame consensus
capture for `force_get_current`, removed "typing on a laptop" anchor, unsettled-during-
motion gating — see [perception-pipeline.md](../perception-pipeline.md) §2 and the
recent commits `60b2b4e`/`2e784c6`). **Nothing to take from pibot on vision.** The only
adjacent idea (#8) is a *posture* one: pibot's vision is on-demand-only, so it has far
fewer chances to hallucinate than a continuous pipeline. If continuous vision keeps
costing reliability, "on-demand-only" is a legitimate, cheaper-to-harden stance.

### Browser vs Android (#9) — the substrate question, answered

The user was weighing whether a browser client (like pibot) was viable for orbit.
Capability by capability:
- **TTS**: Android TTS needs no keys, works offline, and — critically — can route
  through `USAGE_VOICE_COMMUNICATION` for hardware AEC. A browser gives no control
  over the TTS audio device path, so the AEC trick is impossible. (This is exactly why
  pibot generates PCM server-side instead of using browser `SpeechSynthesis`.)
  **Android wins.**
- **Spotify**: pure OAuth + Web API in pibot — works identically in browser, WebView,
  or native. **Not** a reason to pick Android either way.
- **Camera/mic**: both work via `getUserMedia`. Wash.
- **Motors/hardware**: pibot drives an FTDI dongle from the browser via **WebUSB** —
  real but Chrome-only and gesture-gated. Orbit's ESP32-over-station path is far more
  robust. **Station/Android wins.**
- **Always-on**: the decisive one. A browser tab cannot reliably stay always-on
  (background throttling, suspended `getUserMedia`, autoplay blocks, OS tab reclaim).
  **pibot does not solve this** — it's a plugged-in foreground tab, screen on, on the
  author's own machine. orbit's always-on companion requirement *needs* an Android
  foreground Service + wake lock + sanctioned background mic. **Android is required.**

Conclusion: orbit's choice of Android over browser was correct, and pibot is **not**
evidence against it — pibot simply doesn't carry orbit's always-on requirement.

### Same brain, different wiring (#10, #11, #12)

Both build on `AgentHarness` from `pi-agent-core`. pibot's
[`harness.ts`](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/server/harness.ts) is a
minimal, idiomatic use worth reading:
- **#10 local/cloud swap**: `createLocalModel` fakes the local llama.cpp model into the
  pi-ai registry as an `openai-completions` model with `cost:0`, and `PI_PROVIDER`/
  `PI_MODEL` env vars switch the *same* harness between local and any cloud provider
  ([harness.ts:23-63](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/server/harness.ts#L23)).
  A clean blueprint if orbit ever wants an offline/local-fallback dock brain.
- **#11 speech-gated tools**: the `on("tool_call")` hook awaits TTS playback before the
  tool runs and aborts on barge-in
  ([index.ts:380-387](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/server/index.ts#L380)).
- **#12 empty-response continuation**: if the local model settles empty, it injects a
  "your last answer was empty, answer briefly" prompt
  ([index.ts:235-240](https://github.com/badlogic/pibot/blob/main/src/server/index.ts#L235)).
  This is a workaround for weak local models — a useful *warning* about what running a
  12–26B local brain costs in robustness, not something orbit (on Gemini) needs.

### Local-ML stack (context, not a takeaway)

pibot runs everything on-device: llama.cpp (`llama-server`, OpenAI-compat, auto-downloads
pinned binary+model), parakeet.cpp STT + Silero VAD, qwen3-tts with three interchangeable
backends (cpp/rust/python), all as child processes over a binary length-prefixed
stdin/stdout protocol, Metal on Mac / Vulkan on Linux. Impressively self-contained (no
cloud, no keys, ~8–10 GB unified memory) but a large, brittle build surface (CMake,
Vulkan/Metal, Rust, two submodules, GGML op patches). It's a coherent *opposite* bet to
orbit's cloud brain — relevant only if orbit ever pursues an offline mode (#10).

### STT engine — Parakeet vs orbit's Whisper (#13)

Source: the author's blog post,
[mariozechner.at/posts/2026-05-30-shitty-robot](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2026-05-30-shitty-robot/)
(researched 2026-06-22), plus the public Open-ASR leaderboard and the
[NVIDIA Parakeet-TDT-0.6B-v3 model card](https://huggingface.co/nvidia/parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3).

> Note: the blog clarifies pibot's STT runs **int8 ONNX via `parakeet-rs`** at
> "50x real time on my M1 Max" — the committed repo's `parakeet-cpp` (GGUF) worker
> is a later/alternate path. Either way it's local Parakeet, not Whisper.

His reasoning: "Whisper is essentially a batch model... its performance isn't super
great. Parakeet is also a batch model, but much faster." His pipeline does Silero VAD
on 32 ms chunks, an **interim transcript every 250 ms over the last 4000 ms**, and a
final after 800 ms silence — the interims drive his snappy stop-word barge-in.

Independent facts (not measured by me):
- **English accuracy**: marginal difference vs Whisper (both ~2% WER clean). Parakeet
  **-v3 beats Whisper-large-v3** on the diverse Open-ASR leaderboard at a fraction of
  the size. Orbit is on Whisper-**small.en**, so a v3 Parakeet would plausibly be both
  more accurate AND faster.
- **Speed**: Parakeet's headline win — RTFx ~1700, fast even on CPU.
- **Languages — the decision-maker**: Parakeet-TDT-0.6B-v3 = **25 European languages
  ONLY** (no Hindi/Indic). Whisper = 99+. For an Indian-context dock that may need
  Indic languages or code-switching, **Parakeet is a non-starter**; for English/European
  it's an upgrade. badlogic uses German (in-set), so it's free for him.

**Verdict**: model swap is conditional on staying English/European. The more valuable,
engine-agnostic takeaway is his **interim-every-250 ms streaming** — orbit's
[stt-watch.ts](../../orbit-station/server/src/modules/perception/processors/stt-watch.ts)
transcribes ONCE at the endpoint, so it has no partial hypotheses to act on mid-utterance.
Streaming interims would sharpen barge-in/stop-word latency regardless of Whisper vs Parakeet.

### "Pocket TTS" (Qwen3-TTS) — and the on-device question (#14)

Source: same blog post + the
[QwenLM/Qwen3-TTS repo](https://github.com/QwenLM/Qwen3-TTS) (researched 2026-06-22).

He dropped ElevenLabs ("super fucking costly, and I also don't want to send any data to
them"), rejected Pocketflow-TTS and OmniVoice (poor German), and landed on **Qwen3-TTS
1.7B base, 6-bit, MLX** via a custom Rust engine (`qwen3_tts_rs`): "4x real time on an
M5 Max and 2x real time on my M1 Max", streaming PCM as the LLM emits each sentence.

**Can it run on-device? Two different "devices":**
- **On the station (a Mac): YES** — that's exactly his setup (MLX, 2–4× RT, local, no
  keys, voice cloning). A 0.6B Q4/Q8 GGUF is a few hundred MB.
- **On the dock PHONE: not realistically today** — Qwen3-TTS even at 0.6B is a
  transformer needing MLX (Apple-only) or a mobile llama.cpp/ONNX runtime; there's no
  smooth real-time Android build, and it would fight battery/thermals/RAM (~GBs). So
  "pocket TTS" means *local-on-a-Mac*, **not** *on a phone*. badlogic's "local" = his Mac.
  For orbit, "on-device" is the wrong frame anyway — the station IS the device that would
  synthesize, and the phone just renders the PCM (it already does, see below).

**Is it better than orbit's Android TTS?** Quality + voice-cloning: clearly yes. And the
AEC objection — my first draft claimed moving TTS to the station would lose hardware AEC —
**was WRONG**. Verified against the actual phone code:
- orbit does NOT play TTS via Android's TTS engine directly. It synthesizes to a WAV,
  resamples to 16k, and **renders it through WebRTC's ADM** via `WebRtcAudio.renderTtsPcm`,
  which (per the code comment) "plays it out the speaker AND makes it the software-AEC
  reference, so the station's STT no longer transcribes the dock's own voice"
  ([DockTts.kt:241-273](../../node-dock/app/app/src/main/kotlin/dev/orbit/dock/tts/DockTts.kt#L241)).
- So the AEC reference is **whatever WebRTC's ADM plays out** — it doesn't care if the PCM
  came from a local `synthesizeToFile` or was streamed from the station. Swapping the
  *source* to station-side Qwen3-TTS and feeding the same `renderTtsPcm` keeps AEC intact.
  (This is also why the user pushed back: "I would just stream this into WebRTC from the
  server, and then it should automatically do the cancellation." Correct.)
- badlogic, by contrast, has no good AEC on his browser client — which is the whole reason
  pibot needs its JS cross-correlation canceller (#2). Orbit's WebRTC-render path is better.

**Real remaining costs** of moving TTS to the station:
1. **Station GPU contention — the BIGGEST cost, and not free.** Today TTS runs on the
   *phone's* Android engine ([DockTts.kt](../../node-dock/app/app/src/main/kotlin/dev/orbit/dock/tts/DockTts.kt))
   — zero station GPU. Moving it to an MLX worker makes it compete for the station's single
   serialized Metal GPU. **MLX/Metal is not thread-safe**, so every model runs one-at-a-time
   on a dedicated thread, one model per process, and inference SERIALIZES at the GPU
   ([sidecar.py:32-37](../../models/perception-sidecar/sidecar.py#L32),
   [perception-pipeline.md §1a](../perception-pipeline.md) — "two transcribe requests queue
   there; the GPU does them one at a time"). Today's always-on load: Qwen2.5-VL (~3 GB, runs
   **back-to-back ~3–6 s/window continuously** while a dock streams) + Whisper (~1 GB, bursty
   ~0.1–0.7 s) = ~4 GB. The collision is structural: TTS for a reply (seconds of GPU work)
   overlaps EXACTLY with the continuous vision loop, during an active turn — so TTS would
   queue behind a 3–6 s vision window precisely in the common case (dock talking right after
   it looked). Memory is fine (36 GB box; +2–3 GB → ~6–7 GB, no swap); **GPU time is the
   bottleneck, not RAM.**
2. **Latency** — synthesis (2–4× RT) + the phone→station→phone hop, vs near-instant local
   Android synth — *on top of* any GPU-queue wait from (1).
3. **Playback-timing bookkeeping** — today `feedSynthesized` derives playback duration from
   PCM length to drive the speaking-gate edges
   ([DockTts.kt:278-285](../../node-dock/app/app/src/main/kotlin/dev/orbit/dock/tts/DockTts.kt#L278));
   streamed PCM needs the same duration accounting (straightforward).

**Verdict**: a legitimate option, not blocked by AEC — but the cost is **station GPU
contention with the continuous vision loop**, not just latency or AEC. Single idle dock →
fine. Single dock mid-conversation → TTS queues behind vision (the bad, common case). Two
docks → GPU is the clear bottleneck. Mitigations: fire TTS async and accept jitter; or
**throttle/pause vision while the dock speaks** (you're talking, not looking — frees the GPU
exactly when TTS needs it); or keep phone TTS as default and offer station-TTS as an opt-in
"nice voice" mode. **Prototype + MEASURE synth latency under concurrent vision load before
committing** — that number, not the AEC question, decides it.

## Update log

Append-only. On each revisit, add a dated row with both commits and per-takeaway
status. Do not edit findings above in place — strike through and explain instead.

- **2026-06-22** — Initial research. pibot `2ef6b69` (2026-06-07), orbit `60b2b4e`
  (branch `last30days-research-tool`, 2026-06-21). Method: source-read both sides, no
  runs/benchmarks. Net: the actionable items are #3 (barge-in preroll) and #11
  (speech-gated tools); orbit is already ahead on STT confidence gating (#6) and vision
  (#7); browser was correctly rejected (#9). Corrected an earlier-draft error that
  called orbit "app-side heavy" — orbit is server-heavy (verified against real code).
- **2026-06-22** (same day, follow-up) — Added engine deep-dives #13 (Parakeet STT) and
  #14 (Qwen3 "pocket TTS"), sourced from badlogic's blog post
  ([mariozechner.at/posts/2026-05-30-shitty-robot](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2026-05-30-shitty-robot/))
  + public model cards/leaderboard. Method: web-fetch of the blog + web-search of
  Parakeet/Qwen3-TTS facts; still **no benchmarks run by us**. Corrected the earlier
  pibot-STT note: the blog says STT is int8 **ONNX via parakeet-rs** (~50× RT), not the
  GGUF `parakeet.cpp` worker. Key conclusions: Parakeet is English/European-only (25 langs)
  so it's a non-starter if orbit needs Indic; the portable win is interim-every-250ms
  streaming. Pocket TTS runs local on a *Mac* not a *phone*.
- **2026-06-22** (same day, correction) — Fixed an error in #14: I claimed station-side TTS
  would lose hardware AEC. WRONG. Verified against [DockTts.kt:241-273](../../node-dock/app/app/src/main/kotlin/dev/orbit/dock/tts/DockTts.kt#L241):
  orbit already renders TTS PCM through WebRTC's ADM (which IS the AEC reference), so the
  audio source (local synth vs station-streamed) is irrelevant to AEC. Streaming Qwen3-TTS
  PCM into the existing `renderTtsPcm` path keeps cancellation. #14 downgraded from
  "skip (for now)" to "watch" — the real cost is latency, not AEC. (Prompted by user
  pushback that streaming into WebRTC would cancel automatically — correct.)
- **2026-06-22** (same day, refinement) — Quantified the station-overhead question (user
  asked if the cost is free/negligible). It is NOT: the station runs a SINGLE serialized
  Metal GPU (MLX not thread-safe — [sidecar.py:32-37](../../models/perception-sidecar/sidecar.py#L32)),
  already busy with Qwen2.5-VL running back-to-back (~3–6 s/window, continuous) + bursty
  Whisper. RAM is fine (36 GB box, +2–3 GB). The real cost in #14 is **GPU time contention**:
  TTS would queue behind the continuous vision loop exactly during an active turn. Added a
  "throttle vision while speaking" mitigation; the gating metric is synth latency under live
  vision load. Inventory verified via perception-pipeline.md §1a/§8, models/BENCHMARKS.md,
  perception-runbook.md, sidecars.ts.
